question_text: Which of the following correctly defines 'posture' as understood in Physical Education and identifies the two main categories into which posture is classified?
options: A. Posture refers only to the way a person lies down on a bed and is classified as soft posture and hard posture

B. Posture is the alignment and positioning of the body parts in relation to one another and is classified into static posture (maintained while stationary — standing, sitting) and dynamic posture (maintained during movement — walking, running)
C. Posture refers exclusively to the facial expression a person displays and is classified as happy posture and sad posture
D. Posture is the weight of a person's body measured in kilograms and is classified as light posture and heavy posture
explanation: Posture is the position in which body parts are aligned relative to one another and the force of gravity. It is classified as static posture (maintained while the body is stationary — standing, sitting, lying) and dynamic posture (maintained during movement — walking, running, lifting, bending).
elimination_hints: A, C and D describe unrelated concepts. B correctly defines posture as body alignment and identifies the standard static/dynamic classification.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Kyphosis is a common postural deformity of the upper back. Which of the following correctly describes the characteristics of kyphosis and its most commonly observed visual appearance?
options: A. Kyphosis is an excessive inward curvature of the lower back that makes the abdomen protrude forward
B. Kyphosis is an excessive outward curvature of the thoracic (upper) spine that results in a rounded or hunched upper back, often accompanied by forward head position and rounded shoulders
C. Kyphosis is a lateral sideways curvature of the spine that makes one shoulder higher than the other
D. Kyphosis is a flattening of the arch of the foot that causes pain during walking and standing
explanation: Kyphosis is an excessive outward (posterior) curvature of the thoracic spine, resulting in a visibly rounded or hunched upper back. It is often accompanied by forward head posture and rounded shoulders. Causes include poor sitting habits, weak back muscles, osteoporosis and Scheuermann's disease.
elimination_hints: A describes lordosis (lower back curve); C describes scoliosis (lateral curve); D describes flat foot. B correctly describes kyphosis as the thoracic hunch.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Lordosis is a postural deformity characterised by an excessive inward curvature of the lumbar spine. Which of the following correctly identifies its typical causes and visual characteristics?
options: A. Lordosis causes the upper back to hunch forward and is mainly seen in elderly individuals due to osteoporosis

B. Lordosis causes an exaggerated inward curve of the lower back, making the abdomen and buttocks protrude, and is commonly caused by obesity, weak abdominal muscles, pregnancy, or prolonged poor standing habits
C. Lordosis causes the spine to curve sideways and is diagnosed using the Adams forward bend test
D. Lordosis causes the feet to become completely flat with no visible arch
explanation: Lordosis is excessive inward curvature (anterior tilt) of the lumbar spine. Visual appearance: exaggerated lower back curve, protruding abdomen and buttocks. Common causes: obesity (increased abdominal weight pulls the spine forward), weak abdominal muscles, pregnancy, prolonged poor standing posture, and wearing high heels regularly.
elimination_hints: A describes kyphosis; C describes scoliosis (Adams test); D describes flat foot. B correctly identifies lordosis as the lower back curve with abdominal protrusion.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze the postural deformity known as scoliosis and explain how it differs from kyphosis and lordosis in terms of the direction of spinal curvature and the diagnostic method used to detect it.
options: A. Scoliosis is identical to kyphosis and lordosis; all three involve curvature in the same direction and are diagnosed using the same blood test

B. Scoliosis involves a lateral (sideways) curvature of the spine forming an 'S' or 'C' shape when viewed from behind, unlike kyphosis (outward thoracic curve) and lordosis (inward lumbar curve) which are sagittal plane deformities; scoliosis is commonly detected using the Adams forward bend test
C. Scoliosis affects only the neck vertebrae and has no visible effect on the trunk or shoulders
D. Scoliosis is a temporary muscle spasm that resolves within hours and does not involve any actual spinal curvature
explanation: Scoliosis is a lateral (sideways) curvature of the spine — an 'S' or 'C' shape visible from behind (frontal plane deformity). This differs from kyphosis (sagittal plane, backward curve) and lordosis (sagittal plane, forward curve). The Adams forward bend test is used for detection: the patient bends forward and the examiner observes for asymmetry (rib hump) indicating spinal rotation.
elimination_hints: A claims all three are identical (they differ in direction); C limits to neck only; D trivialises it as temporary spasm. B correctly distinguishes lateral (scoliosis) from sagittal (kyphosis/lordosis) and identifies the Adams test.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following postural deformities affects the lower extremities and is characterised by the knees touching each other while the ankles remain apart when a person stands with legs together?
options: A. Bow legs (Genu Varum), where the knees are wide apart while the ankles touch
B. Knock-knees (Genu Valgum), where the knees touch or overlap while the ankles remain apart
C. Flat foot (Pes Planus), where the arch of the foot collapses to the ground
D. Round shoulders, where the shoulders roll forward and the chest appears concave
explanation: Knock-knees (Genu Valgum) is a lower limb postural deformity where the knees angle inward and touch each other (or even overlap), while the ankles remain apart. It creates an 'X-shape' appearance of the legs when viewed from the front.
elimination_hints: A describes the opposite condition (knees apart); C affects the feet, not knees; D affects the shoulders. B correctly describes knock-knees with the diagnostic sign (knees touch, ankles apart).
subject: Physical Education

question_text: A student habitually carries a heavy school bag on one shoulder only. Analyze which postural deformity this habit is most likely to contribute to over time, and explain the mechanism involved.
options: A. Flat foot — because the weight of the bag flattens the arches of both feet equally

B. Scoliosis — because the asymmetric load on one shoulder causes the spine to curve laterally towards the loaded side to compensate, and sustained repetition of this asymmetric loading can lead to a fixed lateral spinal curvature
C. Kyphosis — because carrying a bag on one shoulder causes the upper back to curve outward symmetrically
D. Lordosis — because one-shoulder bag carrying increases the inward curve of the lower back
explanation: Carrying a heavy bag on one shoulder creates asymmetric loading: the body compensates by laterally tilting the spine towards the heavy side. Over time, this habitual asymmetric posture can contribute to functional scoliosis (lateral spinal curvature). The solution is to use both straps of a backpack to distribute weight symmetrically.
elimination_hints: A affects feet, not spine; C (kyphosis) and D (lordosis) are sagittal plane deformities (front-back), not lateral. One-sided loading logically causes a lateral deviation = scoliosis (B).
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate which set of corrective exercises would be most appropriate for a student diagnosed with mild kyphosis (rounded upper back) to improve their posture over time.
options: A. Heavy squats and leg press exercises focusing exclusively on lower body strength

B. Back extension exercises, chest-opening stretches, shoulder blade squeezes (scapular retraction), chin tucks and strengthening of the upper back muscles (rhomboids, middle trapezius) combined with stretching of tight chest muscles (pectorals)
C. Prolonged sitting in a hunched position for eight hours daily to gradually get accustomed to the rounded posture
D. Running long distances at high speed to correct upper back curvature through cardiovascular conditioning
explanation: Kyphosis correction requires: strengthening weak upper back muscles (rhomboids, middle trapezius, posterior deltoids) through back extensions and scapular retraction exercises, stretching tight anterior muscles (pectorals, anterior deltoids) through chest-opening stretches, and improving head position through chin tuck exercises. This approach addresses both the muscle weakness and tightness causing the deformity.
elimination_hints: A targets lower body (irrelevant to upper back); C reinforces the problem posture; D does not target specific postural muscles. B correctly targets the muscle imbalance causing kyphosis.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the characteristics of 'flat foot' (Pes Planus) and identifies its primary impact on an individual's ability to walk and stand comfortably?
options: A. Flat foot means the foot has an excessively high arch that prevents proper ground contact during walking

B. Flat foot is a condition where the medial longitudinal arch of the foot is reduced or absent, causing the entire sole to make contact with the ground, which can lead to foot pain, ankle instability, knee strain and fatigue during prolonged standing or walking
C. Flat foot only affects the toes and has no impact on the arch or sole of the foot
D. Flat foot is a temporary condition that only occurs during infancy and always resolves by age two
explanation: Flat foot (Pes Planus) is characterised by a reduced or absent medial longitudinal arch — the entire sole contacts the ground. This alters biomechanics: reduced shock absorption, altered gait, foot/ankle pain, increased knee and hip strain, and fatigue during prolonged standing or walking. While common in infants (developmental flat foot), it can persist or develop in adults.
elimination_hints: A describes high arch (pes cavus); C limits to toes only; D claims universal resolution by age two (untrue for many individuals). B correctly describes flat foot's arch collapse and biomechanical consequences.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following personal hygiene practices is recommended for maintaining oral health and preventing dental diseases such as tooth decay, gum disease and bad breath?
options: A. Brushing teeth only once a month using cold water without any toothpaste
B. Brushing teeth at least twice daily with fluoride toothpaste, flossing daily, rinsing after meals, and visiting a dentist regularly for check-ups and cleaning
C. Avoiding all contact between the toothbrush and teeth to protect the enamel from damage
D. Using sugar-coated chewing gum as a substitute for brushing teeth after every meal
explanation: Recommended oral hygiene: brush teeth at least twice daily (morning and before bed) using fluoride toothpaste, floss daily to remove plaque between teeth, rinse mouth after meals, limit sugary foods and drinks, and visit a dentist regularly (every 6 months) for professional cleaning and check-ups.
elimination_hints: A is grossly insufficient; C prevents cleaning entirely; D introduces sugar (causes decay). B correctly lists the standard oral hygiene recommendations.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate the importance of adequate sleep for different age groups and identify which statement correctly reflects the relationship between insufficient sleep and its impact on physical health, cognitive function and sports performance.
options: A. Sleep duration has no impact on physical health, cognitive function or sports performance and humans can function optimally without any sleep indefinitely

B. Insufficient sleep impairs growth hormone secretion (affecting physical development in adolescents), reduces cognitive function (attention, memory, decision-making), weakens immune response, increases injury risk, delays recovery from training, and diminishes reaction time and coordination — all critical factors for sports performance
C. Sleeping more than four hours per night is unnecessary for all age groups including growing children and athletes
D. Insufficient sleep only causes mild drowsiness and has no measurable effect on athletic performance or academic achievement
explanation: Insufficient sleep has wide-ranging negative effects: impaired growth hormone secretion (crucial for adolescent development), reduced cognitive function (attention, memory, decision-making), weakened immune system (increased illness susceptibility), elevated injury risk, delayed post-exercise recovery, impaired reaction time, coordination and accuracy — all directly impacting sports performance and academic achievement.
elimination_hints: A denies all sleep importance; C grossly underestimates sleep needs; D trivialises documented effects. B comprehensively lists the multi-system impacts of sleep deprivation.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following postural deformities in Column A with the specific spinal region and direction of abnormal curvature in Column B.
column_b: P. Lumbar spine — excessive inward (anterior) curvature causing protruding abdomen
Q. Thoracic spine — excessive outward (posterior) curvature causing hunched/rounded upper back
R. Shoulder girdle — forward rolling of shoulders with tight pectorals and weak upper back muscles
S. Entire spine — abnormal lateral (sideways) curvature forming S or C shape viewed from behind
explanation: Kyphosis → thoracic, outward/posterior curve (Q). Lordosis → lumbar, inward/anterior curve (P). Scoliosis → lateral sideways curvature (S). Round shoulders → shoulder girdle forward rolling (R).
elimination_hints: Kyphosis (1) = 'hunchback' = thoracic outward curve (Q); lordosis (2) = 'swayback' = lumbar inward curve (P). Anchor with these well-known associations.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following corrective measures in Column A with the specific postural deformity they are designed to correct in Column B.
column_a: 1. Abdominal crunches and core strengthening to reduce anterior pelvic tilt
2. Towel-scrunching exercises and walking on sand to strengthen foot muscles
3. Lateral bending exercises towards the convex side and asymmetric stretching
4. Wall angels, chest doorway stretches and scapular retraction exercises
column_b: P. Kyphosis (rounded upper back) — to strengthen back extensors and stretch tight chest
Q. Flat foot (Pes Planus) — to rebuild the medial arch through intrinsic foot muscle strengthening
R. Scoliosis (lateral curvature) — to stretch the concave side and strengthen the convex side
S. Lordosis (excessive lumbar curve) — to strengthen weak abdominals and reduce pelvic tilt
explanation: Abdominal crunches/core strengthening → lordosis correction (S, reduces anterior pelvic tilt). Towel-scrunching/sand walking → flat foot correction (Q, strengthens arch muscles). Lateral bending to convex side → scoliosis correction (R). Wall angels/chest stretches → kyphosis correction (P, strengthens back, stretches chest).
elimination_hints: Abdominal exercises (1) target lordosis/pelvic tilt (S); towel-scrunching (2) clearly targets foot arch (Q). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following personal hygiene practices in Column A with the specific body part or health concern they primarily address in Column B.
column_a: 1. Washing hair regularly with mild shampoo and keeping the scalp clean
2. Trimming nails regularly, keeping them clean and avoiding nail biting
3. Cleaning ears gently with a soft cloth and avoiding insertion of sharp objects
4. Wearing clean, properly fitting footwear and keeping feet dry
column_b: P. Ear hygiene — preventing wax build-up, ear infections and eardrum damage
Q. Hair and scalp hygiene — preventing dandruff, lice infestation and scalp infections
R. Foot hygiene — preventing athlete's foot, fungal infections, corns and blisters
S. Nail hygiene — preventing dirt accumulation under nails, fungal infections and ingrown nails
explanation: Hair washing → scalp/hair hygiene (Q). Nail trimming → nail hygiene (S). Ear cleaning → ear hygiene (P). Clean footwear/dry feet → foot hygiene (R).
elimination_hints: Each practice clearly targets its respective body part — hair washing (1) for scalp (Q); ear cleaning (3) for ears (P). Direct body-part matching.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding correct standing posture.
assertion: Correct standing posture requires that the head, shoulders, hips, knees and ankles be aligned vertically with the natural curves of the spine maintained, and body weight distributed evenly on both feet.
reason: Proper alignment distributes the force of gravity evenly through the body's support structures, minimising stress on muscles, ligaments and joints, thereby reducing fatigue and the risk of developing postural deformities over time.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion correctly describes proper standing posture (vertical alignment, natural curves, even weight distribution). The Reason correctly explains why this alignment matters — it distributes gravitational force evenly, reducing muscular/ligament stress and deformity risk. The Reason is the biomechanical justification for the Assertion's postural guidelines.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-established biomechanics and postural science principles. The Reason explains the 'why' (force distribution, stress reduction) behind the Assertion's 'what' (alignment guidelines).
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding bow legs and knock-knees in children.
assertion: Bow legs (Genu Varum) and knock-knees (Genu Valgum) are commonly observed as normal developmental variations in young children and often self-correct as the child grows older.
reason: Bow legs are considered a congenital spinal deformity that requires immediate surgical intervention in all children regardless of age.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion is true — bow legs and knock-knees are common developmental variations in young children. Bow legs are normal up to about 2 years; knock-knees are common between 3–6 years. Most cases self-correct by age 7–8. The Reason is false — bow legs are a lower limb condition (not spinal), and they do NOT require immediate surgery in all children. Most cases are physiological and resolve naturally.
elimination_hints: The Reason wrongly calls bow legs a 'spinal deformity' (it affects the legs, not spine) and demands 'immediate surgery in all children' (most cases self-correct). Both claims are false.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the importance of personal hygiene for athletes and sports participants.
assertion: Personal hygiene is especially important for athletes and sports participants because they are more susceptible to skin infections, fungal conditions and body odour due to regular sweating, close physical contact and shared facilities.
reason: Sweat creates a warm, moist environment on the skin that promotes the growth of bacteria and fungi, and shared changing rooms, showers and equipment increase the risk of cross-infection among team members.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion is true — athletes face heightened hygiene risks. The Reason correctly explains the mechanism: sweating creates ideal conditions for microbial growth, and shared facilities increase cross-infection risk — directly explaining why athletes are more susceptible to skin infections, fungal conditions and body odour.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-established sports hygiene facts. The Reason provides the specific biological and environmental mechanisms (sweat, shared facilities) that explain the Assertion's increased risk claim.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following postural deformities in order based on the spinal region they affect, starting from the highest (closest to the head) to the lowest (closest to the feet).
items: I. Lordosis (excessive inward curvature of the lumbar spine)
II. Flat foot (collapse of the medial arch of the foot)
III. Kyphosis (excessive outward curvature of the thoracic spine)
IV. Knock-knees (inward angulation of the knees)
options: A. III → I → IV → II
B. II → IV → I → III
C. I → III → II → IV
D. IV → II → III → I
explanation: Top to bottom: Kyphosis — thoracic spine (III, upper back) → Lordosis — lumbar spine (I, lower back) → Knock-knees — knee joint (IV, lower limbs) → Flat foot — foot arch (II, feet). This follows the anatomical order from head to toe.
elimination_hints: Kyphosis (III) affects the upper back (highest); flat foot (II) affects the feet (lowest). Anchor with these extremes.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following recommended daily personal hygiene activities in the order a student would typically perform them from morning wake-up to bedtime.
items: I. Brushing teeth before going to bed at night to remove food particles accumulated during the day
II. Brushing teeth and washing face immediately after waking up in the morning
III. Bathing and wearing clean clothes before leaving for school
IV. Washing hands thoroughly with soap before eating lunch at school
options: A. II → III → IV → I
B. IV → I → II → III
C. I → IV → III → II
D. III → II → I → IV
explanation: Daily hygiene order: Morning brushing and face wash (II) → Bathing and clean clothes (III) → Handwashing before lunch (IV, midday) → Night brushing before bed (I). This follows the natural morning-to-night chronology.
elimination_hints: Morning brushing (II) starts the day; night brushing (I) ends it. Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: True or False: Round shoulders is a postural deformity in which the shoulders roll forward and the chest appears hollow or concave, commonly caused by prolonged sitting with poor posture, weak upper back muscles and tight chest muscles.
options: A. True
B. False
explanation: This statement is True. Round shoulders is a postural deformity where the shoulders roll forward (protraction), the chest appears hollow/concave and the upper back rounds slightly. Common causes include prolonged desk work with poor posture, weak rhomboids and middle trapezius, and tight pectoral muscles.
elimination_hints: This is a standard description of round shoulders covering the visual appearance (forward rolling, hollow chest) and common causes (poor sitting, muscle imbalance). All elements are correct.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Study the diagram below that shows six common postural deformities with their affected body region, visual appearance and primary corrective approach. Based on the diagram, which TWO deformities affect the same body region (spine) but in opposite planes of movement?
options: A. Flat foot and knock-knees — because both affect the lower limbs in the sagittal plane
B. Kyphosis and scoliosis — because kyphosis curves outward in the sagittal plane while scoliosis curves sideways in the frontal plane, yet both involve the spinal column
C. Lordosis and flat foot — because both involve the lumbar spine curving inward
D. Round shoulders and bow legs — because both affect the same body region and involve the same direction of deformity
explanation: Kyphosis (sagittal plane — outward/posterior thoracic curve) and scoliosis (frontal plane — lateral sideways curve) both affect the spinal column but in different planes of movement. Kyphosis is an anterior-posterior deformity while scoliosis is a lateral deformity — making them spinal deformities in opposite planes. This is the correct pairing from the diagram.
elimination_hints: A pairs lower limb conditions; C incorrectly places flat foot in the lumbar spine; D pairs unrelated body regions. B correctly identifies the two spinal deformities in different planes.
subject: Physical Education
